label: Lie Group
definition: A group that is also a smooth manifold, so its symmetry operations form a continuum that can be differentiated, providing the mathematics of continuous symmetry behind rotation-equivariant and other geometric deep learning.

body.text: A Lie group, named for Sophus Lie, is an object that is simultaneously a group and a smooth manifold, with the group operations of multiplication and inversion being smooth maps. It is the right notion when the symmetries of a problem form a continuum rather than a finite list. The rotations of the plane, parameterized by a single angle, form a circle; the rotations of three-dimensional space form a curved three-dimensional manifold; the invertible matrices of a given size, the translations of space, and the rigid motions that combine rotation and translation are all Lie groups. Because the group elements vary smoothly, you can do calculus on the symmetry itself: differentiate a family of transformations, take limits of small ones, and move continuously from the identity to any nearby element.

The decisive simplification is that a Lie group, however curved, is governed near its identity by a flat linear object called its Lie algebra, the tangent space at the identity equipped with a bracket operation. The Lie algebra captures infinitesimal symmetries, the velocities of one-parameter families of transformations passing through the identity, and the exponential map sends these infinitesimal generators back to actual group elements, wrapping the flat algebra onto the curved group. This pairing means that a hard nonlinear question about the group can often be answered by a tractable linear question about its algebra, then exponentiated. It is the reason continuous symmetry is computationally manageable at all, and it ties group-theory to differential-geometry inseparably.

Lie groups are the natural home of conserved quantities and continuous symmetry in physics, but their importance to machine learning comes through equivariance. When a task is symmetric under a continuous group, such as the rotation group for many vision and molecular problems, building a model that is equivariant to that group by construction is far more data-efficient than learning the symmetry from examples. The discrete translation equivariance that makes a convolutional-neural-network effective generalizes, through Lie-group theory, to networks equivariant to continuous rotations and to the full group of rigid motions. Group-equivariant convolutional networks define convolution over a Lie group so that a learned feature responds identically no matter how its input is rotated, which is exactly the inductive bias that physical and geometric data demand.

The Lie algebra also provides a clean way to represent and optimize over elements of the group. A rotation is awkward to parameterize directly without redundancy or singularities, but its generator in the Lie algebra is an unconstrained linear quantity, so models predict the small generator and exponentiate to obtain a valid rotation, guaranteeing the output is a genuine group element. The same trick lets gradient-based optimization move along the group: take a step in the flat algebra, map it onto the manifold, and the iterate stays on the group by construction. This underlies pose estimation, the handling of camera and object orientations, and Riemannian optimization over matrix groups.

More broadly, Lie groups connect symmetry, geometry, and representation theory in a single package that geometric deep learning increasingly relies on. Decomposing how a group acts on a signal into irreducible representations is the harmonic analysis behind generalizations of the fourier-transform to spheres and other symmetric spaces, which powers spherical convolutions and steerable filters. The throughline is that a Lie group lets you treat a continuum of symmetries with the tools of calculus and linear algebra at once, and matching a network's structure to the Lie group of its data's symmetries is one of the most principled inductive biases available in modern machine learning.
